reasoning:
The service entry point in page-optimize.php (lines 29-32) triggers service.php solely on the URL prefix '/_static/' — no nonce, no authentication cookie, no capability check, no secret token of any kind. Any unauthenticated HTTP request whose URI starts with '/_static/' immediately invokes page_optimize_service_request(). The '/_static/' prefix is publicly documented in code comments and is the same URL prefix used to serve CSS/JS assets to all anonymous site visitors. The gate value is therefore trivially known and requires zero privilege to use.

However, the MIME bypass (extension-only detection via strrpos+substr with no finfo_file/magic-byte check) is only exploitable to serve arbitrary file content if an attacker can first place a symlink or file with a .css/.js extension that points to or contains arbitrary content. page_optimize_get_path() blocks '..' and null bytes, so classic directory traversal is prevented. realpath() resolves symlinks but there is no post-realpath containment check. Symlink creation requires filesystem write access — not available to a pure HTTP-only unauthenticated attacker in a default WordPress install.

Conclusion: The handler is fully unauthenticated (gate_breachable=true, gate_type='no gate — open handler'), making the attack surface real and directly reachable. The MIME bypass is confirmed code behavior. Severity stays MEDIUM because actual exploitation of the bypass to exfiltrate or inject arbitrary content requires a chained symlink/file-write pre-condition that is not independently available to an HTTP-only attacker. Without that pre-condition this is a dependent amplifier, not a standalone critical.
